title: A 250-Year-Old Volcano Machine Finally Erupts!
summary:
Imagine a volcano that fits on a table and erupts with light instead of lava! That's exactly what two university students in Australia just built. But here's the wild part: the idea for this mini volcano is 250 years old.

Back in 1775, a British ambassador named Sir William Hamilton wanted to recreate the eruption of Italy's Mount Vesuvius. He loved volcanoes and art. He dreamed up a machine that would use moving parts and light to look like flowing lava. But we don't know if he ever actually built it. All that survived was a sketch.

Fast forward to today. Dr. Richard Gillespie, a curator at the University of Melbourne, found that old sketch. He challenged two engineering students, Jasmine Xu and Andy Zeng, to bring it to life. They spent three months in a workshop, using laser-cut wood, colorful acrylic, and programmable LED lights. Instead of old-fashioned clockwork, they used modern electronics to control the eruption.

When you see it, the volcano glows and flickers, and you can't see any of the wires or motors. It looks like magic! Andy said they faced some of the same problems Hamilton did, like hiding the light sources so the illusion works.

The finished volcano is now the star of an exhibition called The Grand Tour at the university's library. You can visit it until June 28, 2026. It's a perfect mix of history, art, and engineering—and proof that cool ideas never get old.
why_it_matters: This shows that cool ideas can last centuries and that combining history with modern technology can create something amazing, just like you might mix old and new in a school project.

keywords.explanation: An important person sent to represent their country in another country.
To make something again or copy it.
A person in charge of a museum or collection who chooses what to show.
Shaped precisely using a powerful beam of light.
Able to be given instructions to change how it works.
A trick that makes you see something that isn't really there.

background_read: Mount Vesuvius (a volcano in Italy that famously buried Pompeii in 79 AD) has fascinated people for centuries.
Sir William Hamilton (a British ambassador in the 1700s who studied volcanoes and collected art) was so captivated that he designed a mini volcano in 1775.
The University of Melbourne, Australia's oldest university, now keeps his idea alive.
Article_Structure: WHO: Two engineering students, Jasmine Xu and Andy Zeng, and curator Dr. Richard Gillespie.
WHAT: They built a mini volcano that erupts with light, based on a 250-year-old sketch.
WHERE: At the University of Melbourne, Australia, in a library exhibition called The Grand Tour.
WHY: To bring a historical idea to life, mixing history, art, and engineering.

perspectives.description: The volcano is a clever mix of art, history, and technology that shows how old ideas can inspire new inventions.
Some might say the machine is just a fancy light show, not a serious scientific tool.
Historians see it as a fun way to appreciate 18th-century curiosity and how science has evolved.
